The tail end of a massive storm system that earlier crippled western Kansas had just enough strength to bring a round of snow to parts of the area. Two to five inches of snow were common over the affected counties. However, a band of heavier snow, on the order of 6 to 8 inches, was reported across parts of Ottawa, Cloud and Washington counties. One storm spotter in Minneapolis (Ottawa county) reported a storm total of 9 inches. A gusty north wind of 15 to 30 mph brought considerable blowing and drifting snow. Numerous accidents were reported however no serious injuries or deaths occurred.

A strong Arctic cold front swept through the region on Wednesday the 29th of November. Rain, which had been falling through a warm layer in the atmosphere, quickly changed to freezing rain and some sleet. Ice accumulations from Leavenworth south to Mound City were in the 1/4 to 1/2 inch range. The Atchison and Troy areas had ice accumulations up to 1/4 of an inch. There were a few minor traffic accidents with minor injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One quarter to one half inch of ice accumulation reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On November 30th, a massive winter storm produced record breaking snowfall, along with areas of freezing rain and sleet, across much of south-central and southeast Kansas. Southeast Kansas was hardest hit, where several locations were buried under 12 or more inches of snow, and up to one-half inch of ice. Transportation was brought to a stand-still, schools, businesses and organizations were closed for several days, and numerous traffic accidents occurred, which produced some relatively minor injuries (indirect). The exact number of indirect injuries is unknown. Thankfully no deaths were reported. The weight of the snow inflicted sporadic property damage to various roofs, awnings and carports, but no major property damage was reported. Even so, the governor declared a state of disaster emergency to include portions of south-central Kansas, and all of southeast Kansas.      |Areas of freezing rain and sleet began falling across portions of southern Kansas during the morning hours of the 29th, and continued throughout the day and into the night. Some southeast Kansas locations reported ice accumulations of one-quarter to one-half inch by the morning of the 30th. The storm system intensified over the southern plains early morning on the 30th, then moved slowly northeast across the heartland during the day. Snow began falling across much of southern Kansas during the morning hours on the 30th, and ended by late afternoon for portions of south central Kansas, where roughly 5 to 10 inches accumulated. However, the heavy snow refused to stop across southeast Kansas until late evening, producing widespread snow amounts of 12 inches or more. |Snowfall totals and corresponding counties includes: 16 inches, Neosho; 14.5 inches, Woodson; 13.5 inches, Wilson; 13 inches, Montgomery; 12 inches, Chautauqua, Elk, Allen, Sumner; 11 inches, Labette, Cowley; 10 inches, Greenwood; 7 inches, Sedgwick; 5.5 inches, Butler, Harper.

Arctic air followed very mild weather. Then a storm system moved into the plains and dropped a mixture of snow, sleet, freezing rain and freezing drizzle. Numerous non-injury accidents occurred including a school bus in the Topeka area that slid off the road. One indirect fatality occurred with the icy conditions on the Kansas Turnpike just east of Topeka.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice and sleet accumulations up to a half inch brought very hazardous conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of very heavy snow developed and was associated with a strong low pressure system over the southern plains. Numerous school closings and non injury accidents occurred. Snow accumulated to nearly a foot in a few spots. Gusty north winds of 10 to 20 mph brought drifts one to three feet deep. This storm was unusual to the extent that large differences in accumulations occurred within a short distance. For example, one area would receive only a few flakes while a city only 15 miles away would have 6 to 8 inches. This storm and the ice storm of the previous day prompted the governor to issue a state of disaster emergency for Anderson, Coffey, Douglas, Franklin, Lyon, Osage and Shawnee counties. This ensured state resources would be available to areas most affected by the two storms including expediting the restoration of electrical power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the county northwest of Interstate 35 reported under 4 inches of snow but areas along and southeast of the Interstate reported 5 to 6 inches with nearly 7 inches in the southeast corner.

A very strong upper level storm system tracked from southern Oklahoma into southeast Missouri, from November 30th through December 1st. Gulf of Mexico moisture was delivered north into the cold air, and snow developed and moved across the area on the afternoon and evening of the 30th. The snows came to an end, early on December 1st. The accumulating snow was south of a Tonganoxie to Kansas City, Kansas line. Only trace amounts were observed north of that line. South of that line, the snow ranged from around 2 inches at Kansas City, Kansas, to around a foot at Mound City in Linn county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: See November 2006 Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The heavy snow event of the previous day was winding down and ending early on the 1st of December. As mentioned on the previous narrative, the remarkable nature of this storm was the large difference in snowfall totals within a short distance. For example, most of Douglas, Lyon and Osage counties received well under 4 inches of snow yet a few spots in these counties received 6 to 8 inches of snow. Meanwhile, a widespread snowfall of 6 to 9 inches was reported in Anderson, Coffey and Franklin counties. Drifting was also common in these three counties. Snowfall of up to 11 inches in the vicinity of Ottawa in Franklin county was blown into drifts three feet deep.

A major winter storm caused a combination of freezing rain, sleet, and heavy snow to fall over sections of extreme southeast Kansas.  The frozen precipitation began on the 30th of November and continued into the overnight and early morning hours of December 1st.  During much of the day on November 30th, the precipitation type was freezing rain and sleet, with ice accumulations up to four inches in some areas.  The second wave of precipitation occurred overnight causing large amounts of snow to accumulate over the ice.  Storm total accumulations ranging from seven to 12 inches occurred over all three counties.|The combination of the ice and snow weighted down all exposed objects.  As a matter of fact, some areas experienced disaster as many roofs on businesses, barns, and outbuildings collasped due to the weight of the accumulated precipitation.  17 structures collasped in Crawford County, while ten structures were destroyed in Bourbon County.

A major winter storm caused a combination of freezing rain, sleet, and heavy snow to fall over sections of extreme southeast Kansas.  The frozen precipitation began on the 30th of November and continued into the overnight and early morning hours of December 1st.  During much of the day on November 30th, the precipitation type was freezing rain and sleet, with ice accumulations up to four inches in some areas.  The second wave of precipitation occurred overnight causing large amounts of snow to accumulate over the ice.  Storm total accumulations ranging from seven to 12 inches occurred over all three counties.|The combination of the ice and snow weighted down all exposed objects. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread devastating storm produced total precipitation from 2 to 4+ over much of Southwest Kansas. There was even an area from Garden City to Dighton that had up to six inches of rain and freezing rain! Significant, widespread damage to trees and especially utility lines, poles, and towers resulted from the 1/2 to 2 1/2 of ice accumulation. In an area from Sublette to Garden City, north through Dighton had the most severe damage. Over 60 thousand people were without electricity at one time or another from the nearly 10 thousand of power poles that were taken down from the weight of the ice. Some of these structures were high transmission towers. Tree damage was extreme! It took over a month to clean up just tree damage.|There were still people without electric service a month after th event. Crews with heavy equipment couldn't install power poles because of the deep mud on county roads. In addition, service from the poles to the home meters is not possible yet because of local damage on homes.|At the Dodge City airport, 3.21 of precipitation (rain, freezing rain and snow) fell from the storm. The only other time Dodge City, KS recorded over 3 of liquid equivalent precipitation in a single December storm was December 19-22, 1877 when 4.31 was measured. But there was even greater amounts! Up to six inches of rain and freezing rain fell in the Dighton area! The resulting ice was tremendous with several 300 foot communication towers toppled.|Snowfall of 12 to 18 inches was widespread in the far west with as much as 32 inches reported west of Johnson in Stanton county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least 1/4 accumulation of ice was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second, major winter storm within one week brought a large area of heavy snowfall to all of eastern Colorado, southwest Nebraska and western Kansas. Two to three feet of snow fell in portions of east central Colorado and western Kansas, with snow totals ranking in the top ten all time highest storm totals for many reporting sites. Freezing rain also occurred over portions of northwest and north central Kansas with ice accumulations of 1/2-1, hampering clean-up efforts from the previous storm. The National Guard was called in to provide hay drops to stranded cattle. Interstate 70 and all area roadways were closed as a result of a combination of blowing and drifting snow and significant ice accumulations. Snow accumulations in western Kansas counties included... Cheyenne: 12-16.  Rawlins:  15-20.  Decatur: 6-15.  Norton: 2-5, 1 ice.  Sherman: 18-24.  Thomas: 13-20.  Sheridan: 2-12, 1/2 ice.  Graham: 1-3, 1/2 ice.  Wallace: 20-32.  Logan: 10-20, 1/2 ice.  Gove: 2-8, 1/2 ice.  Greeley: 15-30.  Wichita: 15-20.  Damage estimates are not yet available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the county averaged 2-3 east, 8 central and 11-13 west and northwest. A period of freezing rain occurred before the snow, producing 1/2 of ice.  Damage costs are a preliminary combined estimate for both the Dec 20 and Dec 30 storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving low pressure system produced significant snow and ice accumulations across eastern Colorado, southwest Nebraska and western Kansas.  Snow totals of 6-12 inches were common along the Kansas-Colorado border, with 1/2-11/2 inch ice accumulations further east in the Norton and Hill City areas.  This storm produced widespread power outages and brought down a communications tower in Norton county, as well as affecting many counties with heavy tree damage and loss of utility poles.  Storm totals for each county include...  Cheyenne: 6-9 snow, 1/4 ice. Rawlins:   3-5 snow, 1/2 ice.  Decatur: 1 snow, 1 ice.  Norton 1 snow, 1 ice. Sherman 5-10 snow, 1/4 ice.  Thomas 3-5 snow, 1/2 ice.  Sheridan:  4-6 snow, 3/4 ice.  Graham:  2-4 snow, 1 ice.  Wallace:  5 snow, 1/4 ice.  Logan 2 snow, 1/2 ice.  Gove:  2 snow, 1 ice.  Greeley: 6 snow, 1/4 ice.  Wichita:  3 snow, 1/2 ice.  |Damage estimates are not yet available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to nine inches of snow fell on top of 1/4 inch of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The tail end of a massive storm system that earlier crippled western Kansas had just enough strength to bring a round of snow to parts of the area. Two to five inches of snow were common over the affected counties. However, a band of heavier snow, on the order of 6 to 8 inches, was reported across parts of Ottawa, Cloud and Washington counties. One storm spotter in Minneapolis (Ottawa county) reported a storm total of 9 inches. A gusty north wind of 15 to 30 mph brought considerable blowing and drifting snow. Numerous accidents were reported however no serious injuries or deaths occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wide variety of significant winter weather began impacting portions of north central Kansas. Freezing rain, sleet, and snow began early Saturday morning mainly along highway 183 and spread east during the day.  This led to significant ice accumulations which continued through Saturday night. Many locations received at least one half inch of ice.  The deposits of ice have caused widespread tree damage, along with power outages, and damage to power lines and poles.

Snow totals of 6-12 inches were common along the Kansas-Colorado border, with 1/2-11/2 inch ice accumulations further east in the Norton and Hill City areas.  This storm produced widespread power outages and brought down a communications tower in Norton county, as well as affecting many counties with heavy tree damage and loss of utility poles.  Storm totals for each county include...  Cheyenne: 6-9 snow, 1/4 ice. Rawlins:   3-5 snow, 1/2 ice.  Decatur: 1 snow, 1 ice.  Norton 1 snow, 1 ice. Sherman 5-10 snow, 1/4 ice.  Thomas 3-5 snow, 1/2 ice.  Sheridan:  4-6 snow, 3/4 ice.  Graham:  2-4 snow, 1 ice.  Wallace:  5 snow, 1/4 ice.  Logan 2 snow, 1/2 ice.  Gove:  2 snow, 1 ice.  Greeley: 6 snow, 1/4 ice.  Wichita:  3 snow, 1/2 ice.  |Damage estimates are not yet available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of snow reported, following 1/2 of ice from freezing rain.

On November 30th, a massive winter storm produced record breaking snowfall, along with areas of freezing rain and sleet, across much of south-central and southeast Kansas. Southeast Kansas was hardest hit, where several locations were buried under 12 or more inches of snow, and up to one-half inch of ice. Transportation was brought to a stand-still, schools, businesses and organizations were closed for several days, and numerous traffic accidents occurred, which produced some relatively minor injuries (indirect). The exact number of indirect injuries is unknown. Thankfully no deaths were reported. The weight of the snow inflicted sporadic property damage to various roofs, awnings and carports, but no major property damage was reported. Even so, the governor declared a state of disaster emergency to include portions of south-central Kansas, and all of southeast Kansas.      |Areas of freezing rain and sleet began falling across portions of southern Kansas during the morning hours of the 29th, and continued throughout the day and into the night. Some southeast Kansas locations reported ice accumulations of one-quarter to one-half inch by the morning of the 30th. The storm system intensified over the southern plains early morning on the 30th, then moved slowly northeast across the heartland during the day. Snow began falling across much of southern Kansas during the morning hours on the 30th, and ended by late afternoon for portions of south central Kansas, where roughly 5 to 10 inches accumulated. However, the heavy snow refused to stop across southeast Kansas until late evening, producing widespread snow amounts of 12 inches or more. |Snowfall totals and corresponding counties includes: 16 inches, Neosho; 14.5 inches, Woodson; 13.5 inches, Wilson; 13 inches, Montgomery; 12 inches, Chautauqua, Elk, Allen, Sumner; 11 inches, Labette, Cowley; 10 inches, Greenwood; 7 inches, Sedgwick; 5.5 inches, Butler, Harper.

An area of very heavy snow developed and was associated with a strong low pressure system over the southern plains. Numerous school closings and non injury accidents occurred. Snow accumulated to nearly a foot in a few spots. Gusty north winds of 10 to 20 mph brought drifts one to three feet deep. This storm was unusual to the extent that large differences in accumulations occurred within a short distance. For example, one area would receive only a few flakes while a city only 15 miles away would have 6 to 8 inches. This storm and the ice storm of the previous day prompted the governor to issue a state of disaster emergency for Anderson, Coffey, Douglas, Franklin, Lyon, Osage and Shawnee counties. This ensured state resources would be available to areas most affected by the two storms including expediting the restoration of electrical power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northwest part of the county reported no snowfall but the southeast corner reported up to 8 inches with three foot drifts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very mild Thanksgiving weekend with temperatures in the 50s and 60s, a cold front passed through early in the morning of the 27th. This brought cold air and some freezing drizzle to the area. Many roads developed slick spots. A few car accidents occurred during the day on Monday in which the slick conditions may have played a part in.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Arctic cold front swept through the region on Wednesday the 29th of November. Rain, which had been falling through a warm layer in the atmosphere, quickly changed to freezing rain and some sleet. Ice accumulations from Leavenworth south to Mound City were in the 1/4 to 1/2 inch range. The Atchison and Troy areas had ice accumulations up to 1/4 of an inch. There were a few minor traffic accidents with minor injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A traffic accident near Atchison from icy streets, resulted in two injuries. Up to 1/4 of an inch of ice across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major, perhaps once-in-a-lifetime, outbreak of tornadoes occurred across the area as a strong upper level low moved across the area.  Despite temperatures in the 50s, low-topped super cell thunderstorms were able to generate 28 tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were two on the ground at the same time.  The other was across highway 283.  There was a tremendous amount of dirt and tumble weeds in the air with this tornado that did not strike any structures.
